topic: The Sun’s Corona and Solar Heating
title: Why the Sun’s Atmosphere Is Hotter Than Its Surface

lesson.learn: The Sun’s corona is far hotter than the visible surface beneath it, which is one of the strangest facts in solar physics. The photosphere is already blisteringly hot, but the corona can reach temperatures of more than a million degrees Celsius. That seems backwards until you remember that the Sun is not a solid object with a simple heat gradient. It is a magnetic, turbulent plasma environment where energy can be pushed upward, trapped, twisted, and released in powerful bursts. The answer is still an active area of research, but the leading ideas involve magnetic reconnection, wave heating, and the complex motion of plasma along magnetic field lines.
lesson.simpler: The Sun’s outer atmosphere gets much hotter than its visible surface because magnetic fields and plasma move energy around in unusual ways.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The corona’s extreme temperature shows that in a plasma, heat does not have to fade smoothly outward; magnetic structure can concentrate and release energy in ways that overturn everyday intuition.
lesson.deeperDive: The corona problem matters because it sits at the intersection of magnetism, fluid dynamics, and plasma physics. The visible surface of the Sun is where most light escapes, but the corona is threaded by magnetic loops that can twist and reconnect, dumping energy into the surrounding plasma. Waves moving through the solar atmosphere may also transport heat upward. Solar flares and coronal mass ejections are dramatic reminders that the corona is not a quiet shell at all. It is the part of the Sun where stored magnetic energy can be released explosively. For a long time, the puzzle was that the more distant layer should have cooled, not heated up. Instead, observations showed the reverse, forcing scientists to think carefully about how energy moves in a plasma that is neither solid, liquid, nor gas in the everyday sense. Understanding the corona is important not only for stellar physics but also for space weather, because eruptions from this region can affect satellites, power grids, and communications on Earth.
lesson.funFact: The Sun’s outer atmosphere is so hot that it can reach temperatures many times higher than the layer below it.

people: Solar physicists - Researchers trying to solve the corona-heating problem.
Heliophysicists - Scientists who study the Sun as a system.
NASA, ESA, and JAXA instrument teams - Groups that capture coronal observations.
Space-weather forecasters - Scientists who use corona data to protect technology.
places: The photosphere - The Sun’s visible surface.
The corona - The Sun’s outer atmosphere and the site of the mystery.
Solar observatories - Places where ultraviolet and X-ray corona data are gathered.
Near-Earth space - Where solar eruptions can have practical effects.
years: Early 1900s - Scientists begin to recognize the coronal temperature puzzle.
1960s - Space-age instruments reveal more about the corona.
2000s - New solar missions sharpen plasma observations.
2020s - Coronal heating remains an active research frontier.
concepts: Corona - The Sun’s outer atmospheric layer.
Magnetic reconnection - A process that releases stored magnetic energy.
Plasma - A charged state of matter that responds strongly to magnetic fields.
